title-group.article-title: Weibo users and Academia's foci on tourism safety: Implications from institutional differences and digital divide

contrib-group.aff: aCivil & Architecture Engineering School, Panzhihua University, Panzhihua, China
bSustainable Real Estate Research Center, Hong Kong Shue Yan University, Hong Kong, China
cRajamangala University of Technology Tawan-ok, Bangkok, Thailand

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: Tourism safety is essential for tourists and tourism practitioners. This study conducted a bibliometric analysis using VOSviewer and CiteSpace for 2018 articles indexed on the Web of Science (WoS). It also analysed 7293 Weibo posts between 1977 and 2022 using Python, MYSQL, AI sentiment, and Tableau. The first tourism safety publication on WoS appeared in 1977, while the first Weibo microblog dated was dated back to 2011. Compared to the information posted on Weibo, the annual publications about tourism safety on WoS recorded a stable increment. On Web of Science (WoS), the academic staff and universities produced the largest number of tourism safety posts. On the flip side, the most productive organisations on Weibo are government agencies in popular tourism destinations. “Accident”, “medical tourism”, “environment”, “mediating role”, and “hospitality” were important burst nodes in tourism safety on WoS. “Quality”, “accident”, and health-related words were the foci on both Weibo and WoS. On Web of Science, the top 10 most popular keywords of tourism safety-related articles could be classified into two groups: health (“Covid-19”, “restoration”, “pandemics”, “Sars-Cov-2”, “Sars”, “mental health”) and IT terminologies (“big data”, “artificial intelligence”). It has been concluded that “artificial intelligence (AI)” is more likely to be included in the keywords on tourism researched by academia. In contrast, the public may not know about or use AI in the tourism industry. Besides, the top 10 most popular keywords on Weibo related to tourism risks and hazards were drowning and traffic risks and hazards, such as drowning and traffic risks. The digital divide may explain such a difference: the academic circle benefits more from the digital age than laypersons. It may also be the result of institutional differences and information asymmetry.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: With millions of active users per month, Weibo is one of the most popular social media in China. Information from Weibo is an essential source for studies in science and social sciences. Aside from providing advertisements and marketing services, Weibo plays an important role in emergency management, which is related to public health and safety [1]. The posting patterns and behaviours on Weibo during the COVID-19 pandemic were studied [2]. In a study on Sina Weibo (2013–2020), Song et al. [1] investigated the development of various approaches to pediatric care in the public sphere. Based on Weibo big data, scholars could identify rainstorm disasters in real-time [3]. For example, Shan et al. [4] developed a real-time disaster damage assessment model based on Weibo's data. Furthermore, social media is an important data source that reflects various social dynamics. As social media and platforms have gained popularity, many people have begun to share their ideas about tourism safety via Weibo using smartphones [5], which makes it less difficult to document their daily life through words, pictures, videos, emotions, etc.

Many people think that there are significant differences between the real world and academia. For example, the artificial intelligence technologies that are developed in university labs may end up not being put to practical use. Differences between laypersons and academia have become one of the research topics in the academic circle. For example, Zeng and Li [6] studied the construction safety and health hazard awareness of users on the Web of Science and Weibo between 1991 and 2021. They demonstrated that academia focused more on high technology, such as artificial intelligence, while Weibo users seldom did so [6]. As comparative analysis between the Web of Science (WoS) and Sina Weibo on tourism safety is scarce, this study aims to fill in the academic gaps in this context.

Due to information asymmetry and people's cognitive limitations under the new institutional economics, individual perceptions and foci on a subject are affected by their backgrounds [7]. Policymakers, academics, and laypersons are from different backgrounds and may have different ideas and thoughts on the same issue. For example, one of the previous studies found that the public's risk perception of nanotechnologies was generally higher than those of academics and policymakers [8]. On the contrary, nanotechnology in food and cosmetics/sunscreens was considered riskier, irrespective of stakeholders, including academics, policymakers and laypersons [8]. The “digital divide” that is caused by the discrepancy between demographic data and areas with access to current information, as well as communications technologies, might also lead to a difference in perception between laypersons and academia. Therefore, microblogs and journal articles were expected to have a different approach to tourism safety. Weibo posts are made by celebrities, government agencies, social organisations and laypersons, while papers indexed in WoS mainly constitute academic papers. The results of this study can provide useful information to tourism practitioners and academia towards the conduct of impactful research.

The research objectives include a bibliometric analysis of the targeted organisations with regard to tourism safety on WoS, an analysis of the trend of tourism safety, and a comparison between academic and public positions on tourism safety.

There are eight sections in this study. Section 1 is the introduction, Section 2 is the conceptual framework of the study, and section 3 describes the research method and framework. Section 4 introduces the related publications and information on WoS and Weibo. Section 5 presents the results and a discussion on WoS. A comparative analysis between WoS and Weibo is included in Section 6. Section 7 describes the conclusion of the study. Finally, the research limitations and further directions are covered in Section 8.

sec.title: Tourism safety
sec.p: Tourism safety refers to the personal and psychological safety of tourists as well as to the protection of their properties [9,10]. It is an important issue affecting travellers and destination operators worldwide [11]. Tourism safety is also a factor of critical success towards tourism-led economic growth [12]. Maintaining and ensuring the safety of tourists has become even more important with an increased number of tourists travelling overseas for business and leisure purposes. Nevertheless, tourists' safety is one of the most challenging issues in the tourism industry [13]. Disasters or accidents are the main problems for tourism safety. For example, many tourists lost their lives in the 2011 Japan earthquake [14]. In New Zealand's adventure tourism, falls from height injuries were the most common ones among visitors, and involved accidents of falling from moving vehicles or animals (cycle touring, quad biking, horse riding and whitewater rafting) [15]. In 2003, 595 tourists lost their lives visiting Thailand [16]. In China, Zoonotic diseases in tourist areas attracted scholarly attention. Some scholars studied how to prevent imported zoonotic diseases, reduce their spread and improve tourism safety [17]. Some tourists died from tsunami in southern Thailand in 2004 [16]. In China and Romania, geological disasters imposed safety risks on the mountain tourism [18]. In April 2016, two earthquakes in Kumamoto seriously affected tourists in the area [19]. All in all, tourism safety incidents have attracted scholarly attention worldwide.

sec.title: Tourism safety as an emerging topic in academia
sec.p: Tourism safety has been a point of study for various researchers with different perspectives. Tourism safety problems and issues have been analysed in different tourist segments. Regarding leisure tourism safety, some scholars have proposed a theoretical framework that includes the fire-scenario-related effects, hazard distances, and response time scales of fuel leaks and smoke spread from the cruise tourism [20]. Also, regarding gender tourism safety, lesbian, gay, bisexual, or transgender (LGBT) tourism is an emerging topic in academia. Safe destinations and cultural conservativeness influenced the travel decisions of gay people in Asia [21]. Some scholars examine different ways of adopting a feminist approach towards tourism safety research. Tourism safety and approaches to risk factors were reflected in the diverse activities of women who were aware of the risks involved in tourism and underlying power relationships [22].

Tourists' main concerns regarding adventure tourism, physical safety, travel comfort and experience quality were found to be about wilderness tourism [23]. In winter tourism, leisure enthusiasts’ practice without ski lifts was affected by avalanche hazards in remote areas [24]. Mou et al. [25] found that the safety of the national highway infrastructure and the availability of tourism infrastructure were crucial factors that affected tourists who joined bike tourism in Tibet. Murgante et al. [26] reported that safety was one of the major factors affecting desert tourism.

What is more, the way that tourist safety is perceived plays an important role in tourism safety as they mitigate the risk of life-threatening events in cruise tourism according to [27]. Indeed, “safety perception” is a factor that affects tourists’ motivation for adventure tourism activities to a great extent [28].

Furthermore, ecological, and environmental safety also constitute an emerging topic over recent years. Scholars evaluate mountain tourism's ecological safety by taking China's Qilian Mountains National Park as an example. They proposed an index for diagnosing significant obstacles, including the excessive use of natural resources, massive pollutants (solid waste, sewage) discharged into nature, and extreme concentrations of sulphur dioxide and carbon dioxide [29,30]. Yang et al. [31] explored tourist viewpoints around safety and microclimates and suggested ways to mitigate safety problems.

sec.title: Sina Weibo is one of the most convenient and popular social media
sec.p: Social media platforms are increasingly becoming central sources of information and communication exchange for people [2]. A growing number of people are using the internet to discuss about various social topics and express opinions and attitudes [1]. For example, Weibo was launched by Sina Corporation in 2009 as a microblogging application. As of Q4 2019, it had over 516 million monthly active users (compared to Twitter's 300 million users), making it the largest social media platform in China, second to WeChat [5]. As of the end of the first quarter of 2022, the monthly active users of Weibo reached 582 million in number, with a net year-on-year increase of 51 million, and the daily active users reached 252 million with a net year-on-year increase of 22 million [32]. Characterised as open access, convenient, and efficient social media, Weibo has become one of the most important social network platforms [1]. It is a leading social media for people who create, share, and discover content online and it combines different ways of expressing oneself publicly in real-time with a powerful platform available for social interaction, content aggregation, and distribution [33].

According to the official websites of Weibo [32,33], its popularity is based on the following reasons. The speed of information dissemination on Weibo is faster compared to other websites. If we take COVID-19 as an example, we can see that before the media broadcasted the news about it, the netizens had already started posting information about what later became a pandemic situation. What is more, information dissemination on Weibo is broader as more people follow the news that Weibo users forward. Moreover, new media allow more freedom of speech and expression and the censorship mechanism for media like Weibo is less complicated than in traditional media. Furthermore, the expression of public opinion on Weibo offers useful advice towards emerging social issues. Tourism safety is no exception. In addition, there are diverse users on Weibo, including sports and entertainment stars, professionals, tourists, help seekers, sharers, idlers, etc. Thus, it is necessary to take Sina Weibo as a case study and to conduct research on public opinion on tourism safety.

sec.title: Research method: bibliometrics analysis
sec.p: Bibliometrics has become a major tool for the evaluation of scientific research. By visualising metrics that contain quantitative data about a domain, scientometrics allows researchers to gain insights into the research focus, including institutions, scholars, etc. [34,35]. At the same time, there is more in-depth scientometrics analysis in different scientific domains [34,35]. Previous studies adopted the bibliometrics approach in the safety research domain, including construction safety [36], shipboard decision support systems for accident prevention [37], Arctic shipping risk management [38], etc. The application of bibliometrics in tourism safety is scarce. Besides, only a few studies have conducted a comparative analysis based on social media like Weibo.

sec.title: Research framework
sec.p:
The research framework of the study is shown in Fig. 1. In the WoS Core Collection, the topic “tourism safety” was used to search for the related papers sorted by title, abstract, and authors’ keywords on January 11, 2022, and 3850 documents from the earliest publication dates in 1977–2022 were searched. The document type was used as a criterion to select articles and reviews and those were later separated from the rest of the literature.Fig. 1
Research framework.Fig. 1

As shown in Fig. 1, there were two steps in screening the literature. This study removed any irrelevant articles based on topics and titles, such as medical safety, food safety, etc., leaving behind 2507 articles. Then, the researchers screened out the documents in which the abstracts, objectives and aims were unrelated to tourism safety. This study finally included 2018 documents. In addition, 7293 microblogs were included after deduplication. The keywords were used for retrieving the tourism safety-related content on Weibo using Python 3.6.1 and MySQL.

VOSviewer is a software used to create maps based on network data and it visualises and explores these maps. It is a visual analysis software for business intelligence. The research samples of 2018 documents from WoS were visualised using VOSviewer 1.6.17, including published sources, research organisations, keywords, researchers, countries, and papers. 7293 Weibo microblogs, including their publication time, content, keywords, username, number of likes, reposting and comments, were analysed using Tableau 2021.3.

sec.title: The trend of publications and posts about tourism safety on WoS and Weibo
sec.p:
Fig. 2 shows that the number of publications on tourism safety on WoS is smaller than that of Weibo posts on the same topic. Compared with information posted on Weibo, the annual publications on WoS about tourism safety recorded a relatively stable increase. On WoS, the first document related to tourism safety appeared in 1997, while the first information on Weibo about tourism safety was posted in 2011. There was an increase in tourism safety publications indexed on WoS from 2008 to 2013. While the number of posts on Weibo rose from 2011 to 2014, it dropped in 2015. In 2015, the number of publications decreased to 76. Wang and Yang [39] pointed out that according to the overall classification of the top 100 popular events on Sina Weibo, the most popular events in the previous two years included stars and entertainment, policies and systems, and community and sustenance topics. That said, tourism attracted less attention than the abovementioned topics on Weibo.Fig. 2
Annual publications and posts on tourism safety on WoS and Weibo.Fig. 2

On WoS, the number of documents related to tourism safety has increased since 2016, indicating that tourism safety is an emerging topic in academia. In 2016, Weibo's microblogs about tourism safety also started to increase in number. In 2017, the number of posts peaked at 1468 and then declined to 15 and 11 in 2018 and 2019, respectively. According to the report by Weibo [40], tourist expenses accounted for a higher percentage of all topics in 2016. In 2017, some hot topics about tourism safety attracted an increasing number of user attention on tourism safety, including hashtags #Jiu Zhai Gou earthquake, #travel tips, #visitors opening their windows to eat got bitten by black bears, # Bali Agung volcano eruption, etc. [41]. In 2018, although tourism was among the top 25 hot searches, the topics about tourism centred around complaints about hospitality, airport tickets, and overseas travel, especially on 15th March [42], were not directly related to tourism safety. In 2019, the hot topics mainly focused on entertainment, electronic products made in China, sports events, National Day, etc. [43]. At the end of 2019, health, safety and security were at the heart of the closed and controlled tourism development during the COVID-19 pandemic [44]. As one of the first COVID-19 epicentres, China had fewer visitors during the epidemic [36,45].

Between 2020 and 2021, especially during the post-COVID-19 period when some scenic spots removed restrictions on tourists, the number of posts about tourism safety increased from 132 to 474 on Weibo. The number of relevant journal articles published on WoS peaked at 365 in 2021.

sec.title: Research organisation
sec.p:
The research organisations' productivity, citations, average citations, and average norm citations about tourism safety are shown in Fig. 3 and Table 1. In VOSviewer, an organisation's minimum number of relevant documents was set to 5. What is more, only 187 out of 2635 organisations fit the research criteria when the minimum number of citations for an organisation was set as 2. The strengths of the co-authorship links with other organisations were calculated, and the organisations with the most important link were selected as visualised pictures from VOSviewer. 187 items in the network were not linked to the remaining ones and, the most extensive set of related items was 163.Fig. 3
Research organisations on WoS in the context of tourism safety.Fig. 3Table 1
Top ten most cited research organisations in tourism safety research.Table 1
OrganisationWeight (Documents)Weight (Citations)Weight (Norm. citations)Score (Avg. pub. Year)Score (Avg. citations)Score (Avg. norm. citations)
Hongkong Polytechnic University3498364.1201628.91.9
Pennsylvania State University1263712.1201453.11.0
University of Surrey1960840.9201732.02.2
Arizona State University1260019.5201450.01.6
Kansas State University1359218.2201445.51.4
Griffith University2952533.0201718.11.1
Bournemouth University1350229.8201638.62.3
Texas A&M University2248436.4201622.01.7
James Cook University2244429.9201520.21.4
University of Central Florida2444031.3201618.31.3

The Chinese Academy of Sciences produced the most significant number of documents about tourism safety on WoS (Fig. 2). Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Capital Normal University, Griffith University and National Cheng Kung University were among the top 5 most productive organisations in delivering tourism safety-related publications. Hong Kong Polytechnic University (983) was the most cited organisation for articles on tourism safety (Table 1). The top 10 most cited organisations with over 400 citations were Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Pennsylvania State University (637), University of Surrey (608), Arizona State University (600), Kansas State University (592), Griffith University (525), Bournemouth University (502), Texas A&M University (484), James Cook University (444) and University of Central Florida (440). These organisations were also productive institutions with significant influence in the tourism safety academic circle. The Chinese Academy of Sciences and Hongkong Polytechnic University were more prolific in delivering tourism safety-related content on WoS.

sec.title: Cited countries/regions
sec.p:
In VOSviewer, the minimum number of documents of a country was 5, the minimum number of a country's citations was 2, and the citation threshold of 109 organisations was 64. The most extensive set of related items consisted of 62 items. As shown in Fig. 4, the USA, the People's Republic of China, Australia, the UK, and Spain were the top 5 productive countries publishing tourism safety research. Amongst them, the USA was not only the most popular but also the most cited country in tourism safety research. Besides, the People's Republic of China, the UK, Australia, and Spain were also active in this research area, as shown in Table 2.Fig. 4
Countries/regions active in tourism safety research on WoS.Fig. 4Table 2
The top ten most cited countries/regions in tourism safety research.Table 2
LabelWeight (Documents)Weight (Citations)Weight (Normalised citations)Score (Average publication Year)Score (Average citations)Score (Average normalisedcitations)
USA4428959529.5201620.31.2
Peoples Republic of China3854865402.9201912.61.1
UK1533656234.4201623.91.5
Australia1883259209.6201617.31.1
Spain1262300158.2201718.31.3
Canada1041957116.4201618.81.1
Netherlands33145967.1201644.22.0
Italy90133882.7201614.90.9
South Korea105132589.5201812.60.9
Japan86117068.5201713.60.8

sec.title: Research sources
sec.p:
Out of 815 papers, 69 met the threshold requirements in VOSviewer when the minimum number of documents and citations of a source was set as 5 and 2, respectively. The most extensive set of related items consisted of 54 items. “Tourism Management”, “Sustainability”, “Current Issues in Tourism”, “International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health” and “Journal of Coastal Research” published the most considerable number of papers in the academic community on tourism safety (Fig. 5).Fig. 5
Research sources in tourism safety research from WoS.Fig. 5

Tourism Management can be considered the most influential journal in the academic community of tourism safety. As shown in Table 3, “Tourism Management” was the most-cited journal (5,462). “Annals of Tourism Research”, “Food Control”, “Journal of Travel Research”, “Current Issues in Tourism”, “International Journal of Hospitality Management” and “International Journal of Contemporary Hospitality Management” were highly cited, and thus they have a significant influence on tourism safety research.Table 3
Top ten most cited research sources in tourism safety research from WoS.Table 3
labelWeight (Documents)Weight (Citations)Weight (Norm. citations)Score (Avg. pub. Year)Score (Avg. citation)Score (Avg. norm. citation)
Tourism Management875462208.7201262.82.4
Annals of Tourism Research20112242.2201156.12.1
Food Control2786740.3201432.11.5
Journal of Travel Research2278246.1201635.62.1
Current Issues in Tourism4565987.6201714.62.0
International Journal of Hospitality Management2961048.4201721.01.7
International Journal of Contemporary Hospitality Management2857746.8201820.61.67
International Journal of Tourism Research1646926.1201529.31.6
Journal of Coastal Research3341414.2201312.60.4
Journal of Travel & Tourism Marketing2137018.0201617.60.9

sec.title: Co-occurrence of keywords
sec.p:
A total of 622 out of 10,492 keywords met the threshold requirements when the minimum number of occurrences of a keyword was set at 5 in VOSviewer. Before visual analysis, “tourism”, “safety”, and some other keywords were removed to enable us to see what keywords were included clearly. Besides, the keywords with the same meaning were combined. For example, “covid-19”, “covid-19 pandemic”, and “coronavirus” were combined in one keyword, “covid-19”. As shown in Fig. 6, “management” was the keyword with the largest node size. Other highly mentioned keywords included “impact”, “perceptions”, “risk”, “behaviour”, “covid-19”, “satisfaction”, “food safety”, “quality”, and “health” (Table 4).Fig. 6
Co-occurrence of keywords in tourism safety research from WoS.Fig. 6

As shown in Table 5, covid-19 was the latest popular keyword, with an average year of publication of 2020.9. “SARS” (2020.9), “restoration” (2020.8), “pandemic” (2020.8), “sars-cov-2” (2020.7), “mental health” (2020.6), “acceptance” (2020.5), “big data” (2020.5), “artificial intelligence” (2020.5), and “tourism destination” (2020.4) were also amongst the top 10 latest keywords related to tourism safety. This indicates that health problems, such as SARS, pandemics, SARS-COV-2, and mental health issues, were associated with tourism safety around 2021 and that big data and artificial intelligence (AI) were brought to the centre of research in 2020 and 2021. As effective methods to solve health problems, big data and AI have become a research trend in recent years. According to UNWTO, Jeju Special Self-Governing Province and the Jeju Tourism Organisation launched a real-time tourism congestion analysis service in December 2020, actively using big data technology to help Jeju tourists enjoy their tours without worrying about the coronavirus pneumonia (COVID-19).

sec.title: Keywords with an emerging trend
sec.p:
Burst words are essential for literature content mining when focusing on a particular period. A burst word is a keyword (node) that always appears in a specific period, indicating that it is highly valued by researchers in a certain period [46]. Citation Burst is used to detect the dynamic concepts and potential research problems that emerge in a field [47]. It is suitable for examining emerging trends and sudden changes, reflecting active or cutting-edge research nodes. According to Kleinberg [48], a cluster containing more burst nodes denotes an emerging research trend. Using Citespace 5.8.R3, the top 20 keywords with the highest citations from 1993 to 2021 were generated. The Year Per Slice value was set as 1. “Accident”, “medical tourism”, “environment”, “mediating role”, and “hospitality” were popular topics in tourism safety research (Fig. 7):(1)
Accidents in Tourism SafetyFig. 7
Top 20 keywords with the strongest citation bursts on tourism safety from WoS.Fig. 7

On WoS, the burst node of “accident” was a popular and emerging trend in tourism safety during the first period. It had the highest strength value of 4.67 (Fig. 7) and proved to be popular in the early years of WoS. As shown in Fig. 7, “accident” emerged earlier in academic research and increased substantially between 1999 and 2008. Thus, accidents have been a popular area of the discourse around tourism safety since 1999.

Earlier studies usually linked tourism safety to tourists' life safety and accidents. Accidents are common tourist safety issues. Various accidents can happen during travelling, especially traffic accidents [49], such as car accidents [50,51], air accidents, etc. [52]. For example, Nepal was the site of a string of deadly plane crashes, raising serious concerns about civil aviation safety and passenger safety [53]. Scholars have shed light on the accidents, arranging before, during and after accident safety management to reduce the probability of tourist accidents.(2)
Tourism Market Segmentation and Management

Scholars mainly focused on tourism market segmentation (i.e., “adventure tourism”, “marine tourism”, “medical tourism”, etc.) and management of tourism safety. Apart from “adventure tourism”, which experienced a burst in searches, “Balanophyllia Europaea Scleractinia”, “management”, and “medical tourism” were popular keywords from 2001 onwards. Amongst them, “medical tourism” had a secondary strength value of 4.58. Thus, medical tourism was a popular area in discussions about tourism safety between 2010 and 2014. Below are some active burst words related to tourism market segmentation and management.a.
Adventure Tourism

Accident rates increased when some tourists engaged in high-risk activities, which made the problem of tourism safety more complicated [54]. Adventure tourism, including wilderness survival, cycling tourism activities and other tourism projects [55], was often associated with high-risk activities [56]. Over the last ten years, adventure tourism has become popular in academia in the context of tourism safety.

Adventure tourism activities are generally taking place in the context of a specific tourist destination. Scholars' research on adventure tourism safety issues mainly focused on mountaineering, skiing, diving sports, marine adventure tourism, outdoor wildlife tourism, etc. For example, the climbing accidents on Mount McKinley in North America and Denali attracted scholarly attention [57]. Non-indigenous tourists participating in mountaineering in Nepal's high-altitude mountains (i.e. Himalayas) suffer from altitude sickness, respiratory diseases, musculoskeletal symptoms, intestinal infections, eye diseases and other health problems [58]. A ski tourist suffered from fractures and concussions after falling alone or colliding with others [59]. Diving fatalities in New Zealand were analysed by some tourist researchers [60]. South African wild mammals attacked tourists, causing injuries and a certain number of deaths [61].b.
Balanophyllia Europaea Scleractinia

When marine tourism became popular amongst tourists, ecological safety problems emerged. “Balanophyllia Europaea” (Risso, 1826) is a common Scleractinia coral living on subtidal rocky substrates in the Mediterranean Sea [62]. Kružić and Popijač [63] explored the mass mortality events of the coral “Balanophyllia Europaea” (“Scleractinia, Dendrophylliidae”) in the Mljet National Park (eastern Adriatic Sea), a principal attraction for visitors, which were caused by sea temperature anomalies. Balanophyllia Europaea Scleractinia was regarded as one of the essential indicators of marine tourism ecological safety from 2009 onwards.c.
Management

As tourist risks and accidents increased, tourist management became a focus of academic research. While negligence and mistakes in management make the social environment worse, uncontrolled tourism activities increase ecological pressure and over-exploitation of natural ecotourism resources [29,64]. Tourist safety management is important for addressing tourism safety issues. The research on tourism management mechanisms, legal systems, construction of insurance systems and other aspects of tourism has attracted the attention of scholars. It focuses on the prevention and readiness of tourist safety management, monitoring and early warning, emergency rescue, and post-disaster restoration.

Tourist safety management is a necessary subject to be studied. It includes risk management, crisis management (i.e. tsunami safety measures) [65], accident management, international tourism and transport group management for road safety initiatives [66], etc. The research on tourist safety management mainly focused on tourist destination management [67]. Some scholars have also studied different types of tourism, including nature tourism [68], adventure-oriented tourism [68], intelligent tourism [69], geotourism (e.g. volcanic, geothermal environments) [70], marine tourism (e.g. bathing beaches) [71], ecotourism [72], medical tourism [73], etc.d.
Medical Tourism

Medical tourism refers to travelling abroad for accessing health care services [73]. Modern transportation, network communication technology development, and the accelerating world political and economic integration have boosted the medical tourism industry. According to Health-Tourism.com [74], many countries became tourist destinations for medical travellers during 2010–2014, including Thailand, Singapore, Latin America, Malaysia, India, etc. Medical tourism meets people's health and beauty needs and carries over health and safety risks. Scholars reported that infection outbreaks amongst US patients arose from treatment at an overseas medical tourism facility [75].

Scholarly reviews initiated discussions on medical tourist safety by assessing the research evidence on medical treatment abroad [76]. In South Korea, the critical task ahead was to assure the potential customers of the protection and reliability of Korean medical tourism through continued efforts in advertising, which eventually resulted in attracting more customers [77]. As medical tourism is becoming increasingly popular, more attention is being paid to medical tourism safety research.(3)
Physical and Psychological Safety of Tourists

The emerging research trends covered the physical and psychological health and safety of tourists, as indicated by the keywords of “behaviour”, “health”, “quality of life”, and “physical activity”.a.
Behaviour

Tourist behaviour has become a popular research topic on WoS since 2001. By sorting out the relevant literature on tourist safety behaviour, this study found that tourist safety behaviour before, during and after a trip can be divided into four categories. These categories include safety decision-making behaviour, safety preparation behaviour before a trip, preventative safety behaviour during a trip, and tourist remedial safety behaviour after a disaster or accident. Cheng et al. [78] identified management and behaviour as the two main components of tourism safety. Other scholars studied the “unsafe behaviour” of tourists. Non-compliance with instructions, poor physical health, and littering of cigarette butts in mountain tourism spots were found to be risk factors for adventure tourism safety [79].b.
Health, Quality of Life and Physical Activity

With the improvement in the quality of life of tourists and the changes in consumer behaviour, tourism safety researchers began focusing on tourists’ healthy lifestyles, requirements for a good quality of life, and fitness activities in 2012. The emerging keywords included “health”, “life quality”, and “physical activity”. Health was always associated with safety. Santos et al. [80] indicated that health and safety requirements are one dimension of the global framework of the wine tourism system. Tourism safety and health affect tourist satisfaction [81] and benefit the quality of life of locals and tourists. It is also evident that community-based tourism impacts the quality of life, community and emotional well-being, and general health, safety, and the well-being of host community residents [82].(4)
Environmental Safety in Tourism

“Environment”, “association”, “decision making”, “loyalty”, “China”, “service quality”, “water”, “transport” and “strategy” became the burst nodes and most popular fields of research about tourism safety from 2016. “Environment” was one of the most popular keywords with the highest strength value during this period.

In addition to the unsafe behaviour of tourists, dangerous conditions of the tourism environment are also major factors affecting the occurrence of tourism accidents. Scholars studied the weather patterns related to airports and air routes in the complex terrain of the Himalayas and their impact on aviation activities [53].

The types of threats to tourism safety in natural and social environments include natural disasters (i.e., hurricanes, tornadoes, floods, earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, extreme climate, typhoons and tsunamis), man-made tragedies (including crime, terrorism, civil unrest/political unrest and war) [83], traffic accidents [49], such as car accidents [50,51] and air accidents [52], as well as health hazards, such as SARS and COVID-19 [84,85].

While nature and adventure sports gained popularity among tourists, they also brought over tourist safety problems [86]. Tourism spots may become unsafe because of reasons, such as climate change, overtourism and infectious diseases [64]. These factors are beyond the control of tourism destinations [84].(5)
Hospitality Safety and Application of Mediators in Tourism safety

“Mediating role” and “hospitality” emerged as keywords. “Hospitality” (strength value = 3.35) and “mediating role” (strength value = 3.36) became the top two keywords with the highest strength value after 2018, indicating the emerging trend in further research.a.
Mediating Role

Mediating variables explain the relationship between the independent and the dependent variables [87]. Due to an increase in scholars' interest in tourists' psychological health over the last five years, stakeholder and tourist satisfaction, loyalty, motivation, and behaviour have become hot topics. “Mediating role” became a popular term to analyse the relationship between these factors. In academic research, scholars have studied the variables mediating tourist safety behaviour, motivations to travel, and tourists' approach towards safety over the recent years, which were especially popular between 2019 and 2020. For instance, Biswas et al. [88] studied the mediating roles of tourist health, precautionary security behaviour and media engagement in influencing tourists' post-COVID-19 travel. Sarfraz et al. [89] aimed to explore the role of tourists’ approach towards safety in the choice of a tourism destination with the mediating effect of tourist trust in the context of the Chinese tourism sector. Motivations to travel had a mediating role in travelling and willingness to pay for travelling during the pandemic, and that was reflecting a tourist safety problem for visitors [90].b.
Hospitality

Hospitality search has existed as a topic on the Web of Science (WoS) for an extended period and it attracted more views and searches from 2019 to 2021. For example, some scholars who studied and identified crimes in hospitality have revealed tourist viewpoints on crime and risk, the impact of crime on hospitality and tourism, and the crime control methodology [83]. Hospitality safety includes two aspects, i.e., food and accommodation safety for tourists and occupational safety for staff. In their study on staff safety, Sonmez et al. [91] found that large numbers of Hispanic immigrant workers in low-skill/low-wage hospitality service jobs worked in professional settings that caused disproportionate health and safety risks. These scholars analysed infectious diseases that have threatened the physical and mental health and safety of millions of hospitality service workers [92]. Tourist accommodation safety hazards include crime, infectious diseases, accidents, etc. Popularity in artificial intelligence (AI) allows contactless hospitality services that impact psychological safety, entertainment and service quality of tourists in a positive way (Li et al., 2021).

Tourism development, such as the commercialisation of traditional practices and festivities, the relocation of indigenous people to develop tourism, and the destructive renovation of traditional buildings, may amplify the marginalisation of indigenous people in the community. That leads to the loss of the indigenous culture and their approach to hospitality and increases the sense of hostility coming from the indigenous people and being directed to tourists. Thus it leads to tourist insecurity. On the other hand, a lack of local welcome (such as unfriendly and unwelcoming attitudes) causes negative travel experiences and a sense of psychological insecurity among tourists [93].

sec.title: Cited documents: content analysis
sec.p:
In VOSviewer, “citations” were selected as a type of analysis, and “documents” were chosen as units of study. The minimum citation number for a document was set at 80. Furthermore, out of 2018 documents, only 60 met the threshold requirements. A few items out of 60 in the visual network were not connected to the present study. The largest set of related items comprised of 8 documents with the highest citations amongst 2018 documents. They all belong to the topic of tourism safety management, mainly focusing on the impact of risk and safety approaches. The results illustrated the top three popular topics mentioned above (Fig. 6 and Table 4). The content of the top three cited articles is analysed below, as shown in Table 6 [92,94,95].Table 4
Top ten most frequently used keywords in tourism safety research indexed in WoS.Table 4
LabelWeight (Occurrences)Score (Average publications Year)Score (Average citations)Score (Average normalised citations)
Management134201719.21.1
Impact131201817.01.2
Perceptions126201719.31.1
Risk115201713.91.0
Behaviour104201715.01.1
Covid-199420216.92.0
Satisfaction96201818.01.4
Food Safety82201612.70.8
Quality79201722.61.2
Health78201714.80.8Table 5
Top ten latest popular keywords in tourism safety research from WoS.Table 5
LabelWeight (Occurrences)Score (Average publications Year)Score (Average citations)Score (Average normalised citations)
Covid-197420216.92.0
Sars820216.82.4
Restoration620216.21.9
Pandemic1220215.41.1
Sars-Cov-2720216.92.0
Mental-Health620211.00.2
Acceptance720213.30.6
Big Data620219.01.3
Artificial Intelligence5202133.83.8
Tourism Destination620207.21.0Table 6
Top eight cited documents of tourism safety from WoS.Table 6
Author (Year)Documents' titlesJournalScore (Citations)
Sonmez (1998) [96]Influence of terrorism risk on foreign tourism decisionsAnnals of Tourism Research457
Fuchs (2011) [94]An exploratory inquiry into risk perceptions on destination and risk reduction strategies of first-time vs. repeat visitors to a highly volatile destinationTourism Management221
Yuksel (2007) [95]Shopping risk perceptions: effects on tourists' emotions, satisfaction and expressed loyalty and intentionsTourism Management220
George (2003) [106]Tourist's perceptions of safety and security while visiting cape townTourism Management163
Cavlek (2002) [107]Tour operators and safety of destinationsAnnals of Tourism Research142
Seabra (2013) [108]Heterogeneity in risk and safety perceptions of international touristsTourism Management96
George (2010) [109]Visitor perceptions of crime-safety and attitudes towards risk: the case of Table Mountain National Park, cape townTourism Management87
De Albuquerque (1999) [110]Tourism and crime in the CaribbeanAnnals of Tourism Research81

First, Sonmez and Graefe [96] explored the factors that affect the decision-making process in international vacation tourism. Risk perceptions directly influence tourist decisions. Fuchs and Reichel [94] studied the relationship between first-time and repeat-visit tourists and highly volatile destinations regarding destination risk perception, risk reduction strategies, and visit motivation. First-time visitors faced physical, psychosocial, food safety and weather risks. On the contrary, repeat-visit tourists were associated with destination risk factors, including financial risk, service quality risk, natural disasters and car accidents [94]. After citation analysis, the most cited articles were related to the mediating variables/roles in tourism safety, especially between 2019 and 2021.

sec.title: Comparative analysis and discussion between WoS and Weibo
Keywords of Weibo
sec.p:
According to Fig. 8, “visitors” is the most frequently mentioned keyword about tourism safety on Weibo, followed by “accident”, “market”, “water”, “traffic”, “management”, “sea”, “order”, “China”, and “culture”. Weibo users pay more attention to the tourists themselves, which can be seen from the keywords including “visitors”, “water draining”, “traffic”, “dangerous”, “sea”, “risk” and any other accidents. Amongst these, “sea” was popular on Weibo in 2014, 2016 and 2017 Fig. 9. Regarding the number of likes, “China” and “ice” are the two words getting the most likes on Weibo. Sina Weibo had more Chinese users, and the keyword of “ice” indicated that skiing tourism safety was popular on Weibo in 2021.Fig. 8
Occurrence of tourism safety-related keywords on Weibo.Fig. 8

sec.title: Comparative analysis and discussion of keywords
“Accident” is an early hot search on Weibo and WoS
sec.p: “Accident” was not only the most popular keyword on Weibo in 2013 and 2017 (Fig. 9), but also one of the hot searches on WoS in the early days.

According to a previous study “accident” used to be a popular word in academia in previous years because accidents were imposing most risks to tourist safety. In the past, scholars believed that tourism safety was about the personal accidents of tourists. Most academic studies focused on prior, during and post-accident safety, which is more consistent with our conclusions about Weibo.

On the social media channel Weibo, posts, and comments on tourist accidents can quickly attract wide public attention. After analysing the content of Weibo, it has been found that most of the Weibo accounts releasing news about travel safety incidents belong to Chinese government agencies. Some of their Weibo posts reported that local spots had seen no accidents after tourist activities, warning against accidents or setting reminders to the localities to strengthen safety management measures before the holiday season commences. At the same time, some relatively adverse security incidents were reported below.

During the earthquake in Japan in 2011, people from foreign countries started taking jewellery from people on the street. In 2014, tourists suffered massive robberies in Brazil during the World Cup. In 2015, several accidents took place, including a fire accident in the Royal Ocean Park, and drowning accidents of Chinese tourists in the United States were taking place regularly. The outbreak of anti-China demonstrations in Turkey led China's embassy to issue a travel safety reminder. In 2016, holiday travel accidents were regular; earthquakes happened in Honshu, Afghanistan, Myanmar, Kyushu, Japan, and other places in April alone. In the same year, some tourists were attacked by monkeys while playing in scenic spots, and there were also accidents of tourists where they were hit and killed. In 2017, a boat capsized in the Philippines and killed eight people; a female graduate student at Peking University died while skiing and people were warned to pay more attention to the safety rules of skiing when travelling.

Travel incidents are often discussed in the media. Most of the abovementioned incidents were related to overseas travel in the scenarios, which were mostly natural disasters such as earthquakes, accidents, man-made crimes, fires, etc. The related news was mainly published by local government agencies.

sec.title: Health-related words are research foci on Weibo and WoS
sec.p:
As shown in Figs. 9 and 11, although “visitors”, “accident”, “sea”, and “China” were popular words, “COVID-19” has attracted the widest attention since 2020. The findings regarding tourism safety are identical on the Web of Science (WoS) and Weibo. “COVID-19” was the most mentioned keyword on WoS and Weibo between 2020 and 2022. Therefore, in recent years, especially since 2020, health problems have been closely related to tourism safety for academics and laypersons. Popular health keywords on WoS include “Covid-19”, “restoration”, “pandemic”, “sars-cov-2”, “Sars”, and “mental health”.Fig. 9
Annual occurrence of keywords to related tourism safety on Weibo.Fig. 9

sec.title: “Quality” is one of the hottest topics on Weibo and WoS
sec.p:
According to UNWTO (2022), safety is a critical factor in the quality of tourism. The success or failure of a tourism destination depends on its ability to provide a safe, secure, and high-quality place for visitors. According to keyword analyses, “quality” was a popular keyword on WoS. By analysing the content on WoS, it was found that the research on quality covered all aspects of tourism safety, including quality of service, environment, human activity, infrastructure, facilities, residential living standards, destinations, brands, culture, etc. Cambra-Fierro et al. [97] analysed the impact of what was considered a health risk on a certain quality destination. The Brazilian medical tourism market was subject to tight legislation on facilities and continued the development of medical facility accreditation standards, demonstrating a commitment to patient safety and high-quality care [98]. Tourism safety is critical in ensuring both the tourism experience for tourists and the quality of life for residents are good. Therefore, many scholars have studied tourism safety in combination with quality and there is research available on the quality and safety issues associated with medical treatment in medical tourism that proves this [99]. Under government policies, a “bubble zone” was established in China to ensure tourism safety and quality [100]. Adventure tourism activities are safe and involve a high-quality experience, therefore Chinese tourists who follow the rules are willing to participate in these activities [28]. By comparing the most influencing documents and posts that are related to “quality” in tourism, it was found that the focus on “quality” from WoS appeared earlier than on Weibo. Yuksel and Yuksel [95] suggest that the quality of tourism safety is one of the factors that impact tourists’ shopping behaviour. Furthermore, the most popular microblog on the topic with the highest reposting numbers on Weibo appeared in 2015 (Table 7).Table 7
Quality-related content on Weibo with a larger reposting number.Table 7
Time PublishedAccount_NameContentReposting_NumberComment_NumberLike_Number
2015/4/2China ∗∗[Ten 5 A tourist sites, including Mount Wutai, were warned]The person in charge of the National Tourism Administration said some 5 A tourist sites did not meet the standards of tourist safety management, environmental sanitation, service quality, market order, etc. Tourist rip-offs were common there.10438124
2017/2/2Released by ∗∗[Incredible! During the Spring Festival, 2.98 million Chinese and foreign tourists came to Wuxi for the New Year, the city's tourism revenue was close to 3.3 billion RMB.] within the seven days from January 27 to February 2, 2017, there were no tourism accidents or major tourism quality complaints in the city.424544
2017/2/28Release by ∗∗[@Tianfu New District, Dalin Town, “Three Grasps and Three Majors” to Create a Soft Environment Perfect for Spring Tourism safety]Adhering to the goal of “safety, order, quality and efficiency” and the policy of “safety first, prevention first, and comprehensive management”, Dalin neighbourhood strived to create a safe soft environment for tourism in the “Cuihu Pear Township” scenic spot. In this way, a solid foundation was laid for the smooth development of the pear blossom viewing events in March.32139
2017/10/25Released by ∗∗[Shuangqiaohe Town actively conducts tourism safety inspections on the scenic spots and hotels in Baocheng]A few days ago, to ensure the stability of the tourism industry's safety situation in Shuangqiaohe Town during the 19th National Congress of the Communist Party of China, the local tourism bureau organized a joint inspection team to carry out safety inspections on the tourism industry in the Baocheng Qishiyuan Scenic Spot and Baocheng Hotel in the town, providing a safe tourism environment for tourists.31049
2017/2/3Released by ∗∗[We won't rest until the universe does not restart!]During the Golden Week of the Spring Festival, there were no travel accidents or complaints in the whole industry in Binhai New Area, and the goal of safety, order, quality and efficiency were achieved.282037
2016/5/18www.T∗∗[The 6 restaurants and hotels in Taiwan's Sun Moon Lake overwhelmed by mainland tourists are all unqualified]To maintain the safety and quality of tour groups coming to Taiwan, the relevant Taiwan management departments have jointly inspected Sun Moon Lake in Nantou County, a key tourist destination for mainland tour groups.273750
2012/10/29Cultural T∗∗[Draft of the Tourism Law submitted for deliberation, and forced shopping by travel agencies will be curbed]To diversify tourism products, improve the quality of tourism services, and promote tourism development, it is urgent to enact a tourism law. This time, the draft of Tourism Law submitted for deliberation comprises ten chapters and 98 articles, including corresponding regulations on tourism safety, zero-negative group fees, scenic spot ticket prices, forced shopping, and difficulties for tourists to protect their rights of concern to the people.24140
2017/10/9Ru∗∗[Overview of Golden Week Tourism]Last November, the city's tourist reception and tourism revenue increased significantly. The occupancy rate of guest rooms and the income from house prices were considerably high. Public services such as tourism and transportation have improved, and civilized tourism has become popular without accidents.201010
2013/5/3Sich∗∗[During the Labor Day holiday, tourism in Chengdu had a revenue of over 2.7 billion RMB]During the three-day holiday, there were no major complaints about the quality of tourism services nor any tourism safety liability accidents in the city.1950

As shown in Figs. 10 and 11, “quality” was the keyword with the biggest number of comments and reposts in tourism safety research, especially on Weibo in 2011 and 2022. From Weibo user information, “quality” was a popular topic regarding tourism safety. In China, the government has issued some formal documents on the topic. In the document titled “Guiding Opinions of the Ministry of Culture and Tourism on Implementing the Plan to Improve the Quality of Tourism Services” [101], a high-quality plan for tourism services was proposed to address safety measures, hygiene and safety risks in the tourism market. As suggested in “The Opinions on Deepening the Internet ​+ ​Tourism to Promote the High-quality Development of the Tourism Industry released” [102], it is necessary to ensure the safety of tourism data, fulfil the duty of data safety management, and conduct regular inspections in case there are security risks and hidden dangers. Safety risk warnings and instructions should be highlighted in advertising and sales for self-guided tours that involve high-risk activities, such as rock climbing, surfing, and snorkelling.Fig. 10
The number of comments, likes and reposting about the keywords related to tourism safety on Weibo.Fig. 10Fig. 11
Annual occurrence of keywords about comment number, like number and reposting number related to tourism safety on Weibo.Fig. 11

Many policy documents have been issued to promote the high-quality development of cultural tourism. For example, “Voice of Culture and Tourism”, the official Weibo account under the Ministry of Culture and Tourism of the People's Republic of China (MCTPRC), reprinted “Promoting the High-Quality Development of Culture and Tourism from People's Daily” on April 9, 2022. The tourist system is supposed to promote the high-quality development of culture and tourism nowadays [103]. As a result, governmental Weibo accounts have begun to publish and implement plans for high-quality tourism developments. The content related to high-quality tourism safety on Weibo is listed below. Most of the quality-related posts come from government agencies. As shown in Table 7, tourism quality is also often related to tourism safety.

sec.title: Users/organisations
sec.p:
Fig. 12 shows that China's Tourism Network of Integrity (with 7158 fans) is the most influential user on tourism safety on Weibo. Sponsored by the National Tourism Administration, China's Tourism Network of Integrity aims to promote the construction of the credit system in the tourist industry, the culture of integrity and the development of high-integrity enterprises in China. It is a “tourism safety platform” for tourists to complain, protect their rights and communicate with travel companies and other tourists. Tourists can also record and inquire about the professional credit of tourism practitioners. Therefore, the platform combines a complaint and reporting option for the travel network, a leading management system, a travel agencies' statistics management system, a hotel statistical investigation management system, and a quality inspection and complaint management system (https://s.weibo.com, January 13, 2022). Other users with most followers on Weibo include Sishui Public Security in Jinan (with 19,000 fans), Culture and Tourism Department in Gansu Province (with 36,000 fans), China Tourism News (with 390,000 fans), and Ji Zhou (with 15,000 fans), etc, all of which are government agencies. It was found that government agencies, especially those located in tourism destinations, can use Weibo to raise concerns about and improve tourist safety rules in China. On WoS, universities and research institutes published most.Fig. 12
Annual occurrences of accounts related to tourism safety on Weibo.Fig. 12

sec.title: The connection between Weibo and academia in tourism safety research
sec.p: Social media are playing an increasingly important role in scholarly communication. More and more academic journals have established social media accounts on Twitter, Facebook, and other platforms to share knowledge and publish research results, including their latest articles. This way, collaborations between researchers can be facilitated to enhance the exchange of the latest research results and promote the application of science and technology, thus making it easier for users to raise awareness and outreach to academic journals.

On Weibo, the content of academic research on tourism safety mainly focuses on marine ecological safety, outbound tourist safety, high-quality development and safety of rural tourism, development of tourism safety and health, China's tourism safety report, and tourist safety for the elderly. Below are some examples of such posts on Weibo (https://weibo.com/).1)
The blue book on tourism safety, “China Tourism Safety Report” (2013), has recently been published by an academician from the Institute of Public Security of Tsinghua University and an academician from the Chinese Academy of Engineering.2)
In 2015, China's first Argus coastal observation system was completed. The Argus video monitoring system was launched successfully on Zhujiajian Island, Zhoushan City, Zhejiang Province, recently. The system would be mainly used for research on the dynamic geomorphological process of Dongsha beach, tourist safety, and carrying capacity, and the research results will provide a scientific basis for the protection and development of the beach and the evaluation of coastal engineering.3)
A user conducted an empirical study on June 14, 2016 on the safety needs and constraints of outbound tourism for the elderly in China.4)
During the post-pandemic period in 2020, tourism safety and crisis management against the pandemic, tourism integration and local image in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area became priorities, along with high-quality development of rural tourism. From November 7th to 8th, the China Tourism Research Annual Conference of “Tourism Journal” was held in Guangzhou, where people from academic circles of tourism discussed China's high-quality tourism development and social well-being.5)
In 2021, China's tourism industry accelerated the formation of a new pattern where tourism would be integrated into the comprehensive development layout of the “14th Five-Year Plan”. In 2022, the tourism industry will better adapt to the changing trend of supply and demand in the tourism market to grasp new opportunities for the industry's recovery. With the normalisation of the pandemic prevention measures and ways of control, tourism will develop safely and healthily, and the two-way adjustment of supply and demand will promote the recovery of tourism.

However, the number of comments, reposts, and likes of these Weibo posts sharing academic information was close to 0. It can be speculated that compared with other social platforms (such as Twitter), Weibo plays a minor role in or has just entered the initial stage of promoting and publishing academic research. For example, Nature has a Twitter account to share its newly published articles. The Tweet's report link is a short link or URL (go.nature.com/2CD3ovO), which is more convenient for mobile users than the original browser tabs [104].

sec.title: Artificial intelligence (AI) sentiment analysis on Weibo
sec.p: Some scholars have explored sentiment analyses in tourism safety research, for example, detecting safety issues mentioned in the videos on the world's most popular video-sharing platform, YouTube [105]. According to the analysis of the keywords' occurrence on Weibo, there were more microblogs with positive than negative sentiment. To further discuss the tourism safety-related content on Sina Weibo, it is necessary to conduct a sentiment analysis on 7293 Weibo posts about tourism safety.

Based on the study of Nasri et al. [105], the AI Sentiment from CX Data Science was used in analysing the tourism safety-related content on Weibo. There were more microblogs with positive sentiments than those with negative sentiments. The positive and negative sentiments account for 52.0% and 39.5% of all the analysed Weibo posts (Fig. 13). The most frequently occurring keyword of positive sentiment is “good” (Fig. 14).Fig. 13
Distributions of words’ sentiments mentioned in Weibo.Fig. 13Fig. 14
Tag clouds-all sentiments in Weibo.Fig. 14

“Safe”, “relevant”, “improved”, “comprehensive”, “effective”, “help”, and “rectification” was more influential than other keywords with positive sentiments (Fig. 15). On the contrary, the top 3 negative sentiments in occurrence frequency were “accident”, “epidemic”, and “complaints” (Fig. 16). The total count of keywords about “good” is 1,328, while the total count of keywords about “accident” is 981. The total count of “epidemics” and “complaints” are 842 and 486, respectively. Laypersons focused more on positive keywords than on negative keywords, while the negative ones, including “accident”, “epidemic”, and “complaint”, were the focus of tourist organisations, managers, and government agencies. Thus, these negative words indicated identical results with the co-occurrence of keywords and keywords with an emerging trend on the Web of Science.Fig. 15
Most mentioned keywords with positive sentiments in Weibo.Fig. 15Fig. 16
Most mentioned keywords with negative sentiments in Weibo.Fig. 16

sec.title: Theoretical implications
sec.p: Based on bibliometrics and informatics, this study analysed 2018 documents on WoS and 7293 posts on Weibo with VOSviewer, Citespace, Python and Tableau. Organisations, sources, keywords, countries, etc., were analysed using a science mapping approach. The theoretical implications of this study are described below.

The first publication on tourism safety in WoS appeared in 1977, while the first information on tourism safety on Weibo is dated back to 2011. Compared with information posted on Weibo, the annual publications about tourism safety experienced a relatively stable increase on WoS. Weibo users are subject to emotional variation, entertainment, policies, etc., quickly influencing popular topics on Weibo. Thus, the annual number of posts on tourism safety can vary from time to time.

In addition, on WoS, the most influential organisation in the study of tourism safety is the Chinese Academy of Sciences and Hong Kong Polytechnic University. However, on Weibo, the most productive organisations in posting blogs on tourism safety are government agencies, such as China's Tourism Network of Integrity, the Sishui Public Security in Jinan, and the Culture and Tourism Department of Ji Zhou in Hebei. Most of these government agencies are in tourist destinations. Thus, the focus of the public will be detected by comparing the content about tourism safety on Weibo and WoS. New research trends from universities and academic institutions can be identified on WoS, while Weibo provides the latest information from government agencies.

Furthermore, “accident”, “quality”, and other health-related keywords are equally popular on Weibo and WoS. Weibo users place their emphasis on visitors’ risks and hazards, such as drowning risks, traffic risks, etc. Researchers on WoS focus more on tourist safety management.

Moreover, academic studies focus more on artificial intelligence (AI). Nevertheless, the public might not know of or make use of AI in tourism. On the other hand, the top 10 Weibo search terms were more related to dangers and risks of travelling, such as drowning accidents and traffic risks. Institutional differences in academic levels and information asymmetry due to different exposure to information technology can lead to a digital divide. That also explains the gap in comparative analysis between the Web of Science (WoS) and Sina Weibo on tourism safety, and the fact that academic elites benefit more from technology than the public does.

What is more, according to the AI sentiment analyses on Weibo, the public used more positive keywords than negative ones. At the same time, “accidents”, “epidemics”, and “complaints” should be the focal points for tourist organisations, managers, and governments. Additionally, Weibo played a minor role or has just started to play a role in promoting and publishing academic studies.

sec.title: Practical implications
sec.p: Tourism safety is of practical importance for industry practitioners. Comparing WoS and Weibo in tourism safety can benefit governments and enterprises. This study provided some important information for tourism industry players and the government.

Tourism safety is increasingly related to health problems, such as a pandemic, food safety, mental health issues etc. Besides, academia pays more attention to the role of AI in tourism safety as there is an opportunity for tourists to solve health problems using AI and intelligent tools when they are on a journey.

On top of that, Weibo is more pragmatic. Social media play a crucial role in tourism safety communication by capturing the earliest information about accidents the first time, as was the case in the earthquake in Japan in 2011.

Sina Weibo has played an essential role in signal amplification. It creates sensation and promotes information sharing to a certain extent while facilitating the exchange of different viewpoints and providing early warning of sudden tourist accidents for government authorities. On social media, people conduct human-machine interaction and group communication through comments, likes, sharing, etc.
Finally, it is possible to publish academic research articles on WoS and other academic platforms via Weibo, and that is convenient for users to access that content and increase their influence.

sec.title: Limitations and further research direction
sec.p: There are still a few limitations to this study. First of all, it may fail to include some older people who cannot be Weibo users as they do not use the internet to communicate with others. In addition, the results would have been different if other blogging or academic platforms had been the target basis of this research. Thus, a questionnaire or interview might be helpful in further research.

The research could be extended in several ways. There may be a comparison between Sina Weibo and other social media, including Twitter, Instagram, Facebook, etc. Other researchers could compare Sina Weibo's role in promoting and publishing academic research by using different social media platforms. Finally, some researchers could study academic platforms other than WoS, such as Scopus, Google scholar, etc., to research tourism safety.

sec.title: Author contribution statement
sec.p: Liyun Zeng: Conceived and designed the research, wrote the paper, analysed and interpreted the data.
Rita Yi Man Li: Conceived and designed the research, wrote the paper, analysed and interpreted the data.
Huiling Zeng: Conceived and designed the research, wrote the paper, analysed and interpreted the data.

sec.title: Funding statement
sec.p: This work was supported by Ph.D. Starting Research Fund from the Panzhihua University (No. 035200153); Panzhihua University Scientific Research Project (Economic Environmental Impact Assessment of Rural Ecotourism (No. 035001411); Panzhihua City’s Municipal Science and Technology Program Project (No. 2021ZD-S-1).
No additional information is available for this paper.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.
